problem.passage:
30. Read <A> and <B> and answer the question. [2.5점]
<A>
A phonological process commonly found in English is
neutralization, whereby a phonemic contrast generally
observed is not found in a given environment. For
[ɾ] example, both /t/ and /d/ are realized as the flap
between a stressed and an unstressed vowel in American
[ɹaɪɾəɹ] English, as can be seen in writer and rider
[ɹaɪɾəɹ]. By the flapping rule, the phonemic contrast
between /t/ and /d/ is lost between a stressed and an
unstressed vowel, resulting in the neutralization of the
contrast in that position. As another example of
/ɹ/ neutralization, the vowel /i/ within syllables closed by
(e.g., beer) is produced somewhere between a tense /i/ and
/ɪ/ a lax vowel in American English. That is, there is no
contrast between a tense and a lax vowel before syllable
/ɹ/, final even though this distinction exists elsewhere.
영 어 (15면
<B>
beginning of a stressed syllable, an aspirated stop a. At the
occurs, and an unaspirated stop does not (e.g., pea, tea,
key).
b. In some accents of English, /p, t, k/ in syllable final
position are realized as a glottal stop (e.g., tip, pit, kick).
/ɪ/ /ɛ/ c. In African American English, and are pronounced
the same before nasal consonants (e.g., pin, pen).
Which of the following lists all and only the case(s) in <B>
that show(s) the phonological process described in <A>?
a a, b a, b, c ① ② ③
b, c c ④ ⑤
